summary: Gruber responds to criticism of his suggestion that Nintendo should make iOS games, clarifying he never meant they should abandon hardware entirely. He argues Nintendo's hardware is falling behind the state of the art — resistive touchscreens, low resolution — and the 3DS's 23% sales decline from the DS is a dangerous trendline. Drawing analogies to Disney's embrace of television and Apple's release of iTunes for Windows, he contends Nintendo should view iOS as an opportunity to expand reach rather than a threat. He compares Nintendo's complacency to RIM's pre-collapse confidence and proposes a concrete plan: release two premium iOS games designed natively for touch, price them at $15-20, and simultaneously develop technically competitive next-gen hardware.
key_points: Nintendo's hardware has fallen critically behind — resistive touchscreens and low resolutions in 2013 feel like museum relics compared to any modern smartphone
The 3DS's 23% unit decline from the DS is a dangerous trendline that Nintendo itself acknowledges, and another similar drop would be fatal
The Apple-licensing analogy doesn't hold: Apple releasing iTunes for Windows and iWork web apps shows even Apple goes to where users are when it makes strategic sense
Disney's embrace of television over other studios' resistance is the better analogy — Nintendo should see iOS as expansion opportunity, not existential threat
Nintendo's complacency mirrors RIM's: BlackBerry was still growing market share in 2007-2009 while the ground was shifting beneath them
money_quotes: Nintendo should expand to start making games optimized for these devices — in the short term as opportunity to sell more games, in the long term as a hedge for the possibility that the company will no longer be able to compete at all in hardware.
Resistive touchscreens in 2013 feel like relics from a museum — retro technology on brand-new devices.
They need to catch up to the state of the art, or they're going to lose today's kids. My generation has nostalgia for and loyalty to Nintendo; kids don't.
Even Apple sometimes goes to where the users are, rather than demanding the users come to them.
He saw television as an opportunity. A way to expand his company's reach. That, to me, is how I wish Nintendo saw iOS gaming.
It seems far more likely that Nintendo can produce amazing games for iOS than that they can produce technically competitive handheld hardware. I see no harm in trying both.
The same arguments about physical buttons, the same loyal fan base, the same 'sales aren't yet in steep decline' charts and graphs.

Everyone agrees that the best thing about Nintendo's platforms are Nintendo's own first-party games. With Apple, on the other hand, there are a lot of people who think the worst thing about iOS are Apple's own first-party apps.
key_insight: Nintendo should treat iOS the way Disney treated television and Apple treated Windows — as a massive expansion opportunity rather than a threat to protect against — because their hardware is already falling behind and the trendline is unmistakably downward.
